content.Title: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Guideline for Coronary Artery Revascularization
content.Description: A Report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Joint Committee on Clinical Practice Guidelines.
content.Committee: American College of Cardiology Policy Approval Committee, the American Heart Association Science Advisory and Coordinating Committee, the American College of Cardiology Science and Quality Committee, and the Society for Cardiovascular Angiography and Interventions Executive Committee.
content.Publication: This article has been published in Circulation and is available on the websites of the American College of Cardiology and the American Heart Association.

content.Title: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Coronary Revascularization Guideline
content.Aim: The guideline for coronary artery revascularization replaces the 2011 coronary artery bypass graft surgery and the 2011 and 2015 percutaneous coronary intervention guidelines. It provides a patient-centric approach to guide clinicians in the treatment of patients with significant coronary artery disease undergoing coronary revascularization as well as the supporting documentation to encourage their use.
content.Methods: A comprehensive literature search was conducted from May 2019 to September 2019, encompassing studies, reviews, and other evidence conducted on human subjects published in English from PubMed, EMBASE, the Cochrane Collaboration, CINAHL Complete, and other relevant databases. Additional relevant studies, published through May 2021, were also considered.
content.Structure: Coronary artery disease remains a leading cause of morbidity and mortality globally. Coronary revascularization is an important therapeutic option when managing patients with coronary artery disease. The 2021 coronary artery revascularization guideline provides recommendations based on contemporary evidence for the treatment of these patients. The recommendations present an evidence-based approach to managing patients with coronary artery disease who are being considered for coronary revascularization, with the intent to improve quality of care and align with patients’ interests.

Preprocedural Assessment and the Heart T.Heart Team: Multidisciplinary team involved in evaluating patients for heart procedures.
Preprocedural Assessment and the Heart T.Predicting Risk with CABG: Assessment methods for determining risk associated with coronary artery bypass grafting.

Defining Lesion Severity.Angiography: Used to define anatomy and assess lesion severity.
Defining Lesion Severity.SYNTAX Score: Tool to calculate coronary artery lesion complexity using PCI and surgery.

Revascularization in STEMI.Infarct Artery: Revascularization prioritized for patients with STEMI.

Revascularization in NSTE-ACS.Coronary Angiography: Used for revascularization guidance in NSTE-ACS.

Revascularization in SIHD.Medical Therapy: Used to enhance survival and reduce cardiovascular events in SIHD.

take_home_messages: Treatment decisions should be based on clinical indications, and efforts to reduce disparities of care are warranted.
A multidisciplinary Heart Team approach is recommended for uncertain treatment strategies.
Surgical revascularization is indicated to improve survival in patients with significant left main disease.
Updated trials suggest a survival benefit with revascularization in certain patient groups.
Radial artery is preferred as a surgical revascularization conduit.

content.saphenous vein conduit use: Recommended for bypass to second most important target vessel with significant stenosis after the left anterior descending coronary artery.
content.radial artery access: Recommended for patients undergoing percutaneous intervention with acute coronary syndrome or stable ischemic heart disease.
content.short duration of dual antiplatelet ther: Recommended after percutaneous revascularization in patients with stable ischemic heart disease.
content.staged percutaneous intervention: Considered for a significantly stenosed non-culprit artery in patients with ST-segment–elevation myocardial infarction.
content.revascularization decisions in diabetes: Optimized by the use of a Heart Team approach.
content.treatment decisions based on surgical ri: Should include the calculation of a patient’s surgical risk with the SYNTAX score.
content.clinical practice guidelines: Provide recommendations for patients with or at risk of developing cardiovascular disease (CVD).
content.implementation of guidelines: Effective when followed by both practitioners and patients.
content.guideline updates: Continuously reviewed by the ACC/AHA Joint Committee in response to evolving technologies.

content.Guideline Review Process: Recommendations are updated dynamically with new data and reviewed by the writing committee and experts. The terms 'full revision' and 'focused update' will be phased out.
content.Committee Structure: The Joint Committee ensures diverse representation in the guideline writing committee, including different geographic regions, sexes, races, and clinical practice settings.
content.Relationships and Biases: The ACC and AHA have rigorous policies to prevent bias and improper influence. Comprehensive disclosure information is available.
content.Evidence Review: Recommendations are based on the latest evidence, including systematic reviews, randomized trials, and expert opinion. Evidence review involves a structured process with a broad literature search.
content.Specific Areas of Focus: The guideline addresses percutaneous coronary intervention, coronary artery bypass graft surgery, and other related procedures. Drug recommendations and management therapies are included.

content.Table Description: This table applies ACC/AHA Class of Recommendation and Level of Evidence to clinical strategies, interventions, treatments, or diagnostic testing in patient care, updated as of May 2019.

Classes of Recommendation.Class 1 (Strong): Benefit >>> Risk. Suggested phrases recommend treatments be performed as beneficial.
Classes of Recommendation.Class 2a (Moderate): Benefit >> Risk. Suggestions include treatments that are reasonable and can be beneficial.
Classes of Recommendation.Class 2b (Weak): Benefit ≥ Risk. Considerations for treatments that may be reasonable or uncertain in effectiveness.

Class 3 (No Benefit or Harm).Moderate: Benefit = Risk. Treatments are not recommended or useful.
Class 3 (No Benefit or Harm).Strong: Risk > Benefit. Treatments are potentially harmful and should not be performed.

Levels of Evidence.Level A: High-quality evidence from more than one RCT.
Levels of Evidence.Level B-R (Randomized): Moderate-quality evidence from one or more RCTs.
Levels of Evidence.Level C-LD (Limited Data): Data from observational or registry studies.

content.Additional Notes: Recommendations can vary by outcomes specified. The method of assessing quality is evolving.

content.GuidelineScope: The 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Guideline for Coronary Artery Revascularization updates and consolidates the 2011 CABG and the 2011 and 2015 PCI guidelines. It includes sections on revascularization from the 2012 SIHD guideline, the 2013 STEMI guideline, and the 2014 NSTEMI guideline. The focus is on a patient-centric approach using modern treatment options.
content.TargetAudience: Cardiovascular clinicians involved in the care of patients for whom revascularization is considered or indicated.
content.Recommendations: Updated with latest evidence to improve clinical outcomes, aiming for shared decision-making with patients.
content.Level of Evidence: Based on type, quantity, and consistency of data from clinical trials.

content.Title: Improving Equity of Care in Revascularization and Shared Decision-Making
content.Recommendation: In patients who require coronary revascularization, treatment decisions should be based on clinical indication, regardless of sex or race or ethnicity, and efforts to reduce disparities of care are warranted.
content.Synopsis: Health disparities by sex and race are evident in cardiovascular disease in the United States, and social factors are strongly associated with cardiovascular health outcomes. Differences in access to care and outcomes persist due to sociodemographic characteristics. Despite controlling for baseline factors, studies generally show similar outcomes for revascularization across sexes and races. Emphasis is placed on equitable treatment based on patient’s clinical characteristics, with no distinction by sex, race, or ethnicity.

content.Title: Shared Decision-Making and Informed Consent

Recommendations.Description: In patients undergoing revascularization, decisions should be patient-centered—considerate of patient’s preferences and goals, cultural beliefs, health literacy, and social determinants of health—and made in collaboration with the patient's support system.

In patients undergoing coronary angiography or revascularization, adequate information about benefits, risks, therapeutic consequences, and potential alternatives in the performance of percutaneous and surgical myocardial revascularization should be given, when feasible, with sufficient time for informed decision-making to improve clinical outcomes.

Key Points: Shared decision-making is a collaborative approach with unbiased, evidence-based information to align with patient's values and preferences.
Important fundamentals for shared decision-making include understanding social determinants of health, health literacy, and communication employing patient-centered language.
Patients should be educated about treatment options using online modules, decision aids, or videos.
Knowledge of risks and benefits as well as procedure-related and long-term outcomes is crucial for informed decision-making.
Acknowledgment of the patient's right to decline or accept recommended treatments is necessary.
Patient Education: The need for continued medical therapy with or without revascularization should be discussed.
Lifestyle modification and secondary prevention strategies are key components of patient education.
Challenges may arise when scientific data supports a treatment, but the patient prefers an alternative.

content.Title: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Coronary Revascularization Guideline
content.Informed Consent: Clinician provides the best available evidence for treatment options, including the risks and benefits of each option.
content.Patient-Centered Care: Treatment and care options take into consideration individual values and preferences.
content.Shared Decision-Making: A collaborative decision about treatment or care is documented and shared with relevant stakeholders.

Patient-Centered Care: Assess a patient's ability to understand complex health information.
Elicit and respect cultural, racial, ethnic, or religious preferences and values.
Evaluate social determinants of health (education, income, access to health care).
Discuss treatment alternatives and how each affects the patient's quality of life.
Shared Decision-Making: Encourage questions and explain the patient's role in the decision-making partnership.
Clearly and accurately communicate the potential risks and benefits of a particular procedure and alternative treatments.
Ensure that patients have a key role in deciding what revascularization approach is appropriate.
Use shared decision aids (e.g., Alphabetical List of Decision Aids by Health Topic, Ottawa Hospital Research Institute, SHARE Approach Curriculum Tools, Agency for Health care Research and Quality).
Spend sufficient time to engage in shared decision-making; allow for a second opinion.
Work with a chaplain, social worker, or other team members to facilitate shared decision-making.
Encourage patients to share their fears, stress, or other emotions, and address appropriately.
Negotiate decision in partnership with the patient and family members.
Respect patient's autonomy to decline recommended treatment.

content.Title: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Coronary Revascularization Guideline
Details: Use plain language, avoiding jargon, and adopt the patient's words; integrate pictures to teach.
Document teach-back of patient's knowledge and understanding.
Conduct conversations with a trained interpreter, as needed.
Provide patient-specific short- and long-term risks, benefits, and alternative treatments.
Provide unbiased, evidence-based, reliable, accessible, and relevant information to patient.

Discuss specific risks and benefits with regard to survival, relief of angina, quality of life, and potential additional intervention, as well as uncertainties associated with different treatment strategies.
Provide patient time to reflect on the trade-offs imposed by the outcome estimates.
Provide information on the level of operator expertise, volume of the facility, and local results in the performance of coronary revascularization options.
Clearly inform of the need for continued medical therapy and lifestyle modifications.
content.Section: 3. Preprocedural Assessment and the Heart Team

Recommendation.Details: In patients for whom the optimal treatment strategy is unclear, a Heart Team approach that includes representatives from interventional cardiology, cardiac surgery, and clinical cardiology is recommended to improve patient outcomes.

content.Synopsis: A multidisciplinary Heart Team, involving the cardiologist, cardiac surgeon, and other specialists, is critical for revascularization decision-making. Randomized trials comparing PCI and CABG led to the Heart Team approach, emphasizing consensus for optimal revascularization. Conditions for Heart Team involvement include complex coronary disease, combined conditions impacting revascularization success, and other impactful clinical or social statuses.
Recommendation-Specific Supporting Text: Observational studies using the Heart Team have included interventional cardiology, cardiac surgery, and noninvasive cardiologists.
Additional input from primary physicians, palliative care, critical care, anesthesiology, and imaging specialists.
Studies show favorable outcomes with the Heart Team in cases of unprotected left main disease, triple-vessel disease, or diseases involving the proximal LAD artery, such as in diabetes.

Coronary Anatomy: High anatomic complexity (e.g., bifurcation disease, high SYNTAX score)
Patient Factors: Inability or unwillingness to adhere to DAPT

content.section: 3.2. Predicting Patient Risk of Death With CABG
content.recommendation: In patients who are being considered for CABG, calculation of the STS risk score is recommended to help stratify patient risk.
content.synopsis: The STS risk score predicts adverse outcomes such as death, renal failure, and prolonged ventilation in patients undergoing CABG. It is periodically updated and compares to EuroSCORE II for predicting adverse outcomes.
content.supporting_text: The STS risk score has been validated in several studies and demonstrates predictive value. It is preferred over EuroSCORE II for higher mortality risk patients. STS and EuroSCORE II don't fully account for cirrhosis, frailty, and malnutrition.
content.method: Coronary angiography is used to define coronary anatomy and stenosis severity, guiding revascularization strategies.

content.recommendation: In patients with multivessel CAD, an assessment of CAD complexity, such as the SYNTAX score, may be useful to guide revascularization.
content.anatomic complexity: Factors such as completeness of revascularization, predicted risk of death, and other outcomes are critical for determining revascularization type.
content.syntax score: Derived from the SYNTAX trial, used to grade anatomic complexity in patients with multivessel disease. It predicts long-term major adverse events and has been validated in studies.
content.syntax score 2020: Retrospectively developed to incorporate clinical variables besides anatomic ones. Demonstrates prediction of adverse events post-revascularization.

content.section: 4.3. Use of Coronary Physiology to Guide Revascularization With PCI

recommendations.text: In patients with angina or an anginal equivalent, undocumented ischemia, and angiographically intermediate stenoses, the use of fractional flow reserve (FFR) or instantaneous wave-free ratio (iFR) is recommended to guide the decision to proceed with PCI.
In stable patients with angiographically intermediate stenoses and FFR >0.80 or iFR >0.89, PCI should not be performed.

content.synopsis: FFR and iFR are physiological methods to assess lesion significance. FFR is defined as the ratio of maximal blood flow in a region distal to a lesion compared with normal maximal blood flow of an artery. iFR is the instantaneous wave-free ratio of coronary pressure distal to the coronary lesion to the aortic pressure, which avoids the need for adenosine. The FAME 2 trial tested PCI strategy for lesions with abnormal FFR.
keyPoints: FFR and iFR have been studied in endpoints of death, MI, or repeat revascularization.
Recruitment for FAME 2 was stopped due to benefit from reduced ischemia-driven revascularization.
FFR use in surgical revascularization needs more investigation.
Less distal anastomoses were reported in trials comparing CABG with FFR-guided versus angiogram-guided revascularization.
recommendationSpecificSupportiveText: PCI for stenosis ≥50% with abnormal FFR reduced composite risk versus PCI guided by angiography.
Trials show iFR-guided PCI noninferior to FFR-guided PCI.
Lower procedure-related risk with iFR versus FFR.

content.Synopsis: Intravascular ultrasound (IVUS) provides anatomical details beyond traditional coronary angiography and is useful for assessing the left main artery. It offers higher resolution images, aiding in precise lesion evaluation. The text highlights IVUS's role in treating lesions with minimal lumen areas and comparing its findings with fractional flow reserve (FFR) values.
Recommendations: In patients with intermediate stenosis of the left main artery, IVUS is reasonable for assessing lesion severity.
In patients with STEMI and ischemic symptoms for less than 12 hours, PCI should be performed to improve survival.
In patients with STEMI and complications, CABG or PCI is recommended to improve survival, regardless of time delays from MI onset.
In patients with STEMI who show failed reperfusion after fibrinolytic therapy, PCI of the infarct artery should be performed.
For patients treated with fibrinolytic therapy, subsequent angiography and potential PCI help to improve clinical outcomes.

Sections.Heading: Continued
Points: In patients with STEMI who are stable and presenting 12 to 24 hours after symptom onset, PCI is recommended to improve clinical outcomes.
In patients with STEMI in whom PCI is not feasible or successful, CABG can be effective.
In patients with STEMI complicated by ongoing ischemia, PCI can improve outcomes.
In asymptomatic stable patients with STEMI with a totally occluded artery, PCI should not be performed.
In patients with STEMI, emergency CABG should not be performed after failed PCI unless necessary.

Sections.Heading: Synopsis
Sections.Summary: Immediate reperfusion therapy improves outcomes in STEMI patients. Primary PCI is superior to fibrinolytic therapy. Use CABG with caution. Mechanical support devices may improve outcomes.

Sections.Heading: Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text
Sections.Summary: Primary PCI reduces mortality and major complications. Early revascularization benefits patients with STEMI complicated by cardiogenic shock.

content.recommendation_note: The algorithm does not cover every patient scenario and clinicians are advised to use a Heart Team approach. Lack of data may limit formal recommendations.
additional_study_notes: Studies indicate reduced bleeding with half-dose tenecteplase in patients >75 years of age.
Benefits of early PCI were supported by trials showing reduced rates of death or infarction.

General Information.Study Context: Invasive coronary stenting study compared with conservative strategy.
PCI Recommendations: Delayed PCI of an infarct artery beyond 24 hours should be considered only in patients with a patent artery.
Emergency CABG should be considered for patients with ongoing ischemia and a large area of myocardium at risk.
Outcomes: Invasive strategy showed lower ventricular infarct size and mortality rates.
No RCTs examining benefit of PCI in STEMI beyond 12 hours.
COR/LOE Recommendations: Staged PCI recommended for non-infarct artery stenosis in stable patients with multivessel disease.
Elective CABG reasonable for complex multivessel disease.
PCI of non-infarct artery may be considered to reduce event rates.

content.Summary: The guideline discusses the optimal revascularization strategies for patients with STEMI and multivessel CAD. It emphasizes the use of a Heart Team approach to tailor treatment methods such as multivessel PCI or CABG, and highlights findings from various trials.

Clinical Guidelines.Practice: Heart Team approach is utilized to determine optimal revascularization strategy in STEMI and multivessel CAD.
Details: Multivessel PCI at the time of primary PCI is recommended.
PCI of the infarct artery only is associated with a reduction in risk compared to non-infarct artery treatments.
Elective CABG remains an appropriate option after PCI of infarct artery.
Staged PCI is noted for specific patient conditions.

content.title: Revascularization of Noninfarct-Related Coronary Artery Lesions in Patients With STEMI
content.description: Flowchart guidance for revascularization in patients with ST-segment-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) and noninfarct-related coronary artery issues.
key_points: Assess for cardiogenic shock in patients with STEMI and successful PCI.
Revascularization options depend on presence of low-risk patient profiles and lesion complexity.
Routine revascularization of non-culprit artery indicated if cardiogenic shock is present.
Deferred revascularization in certain non-complex cases after evaluating myocardium at risk and comorbidities.
Decision pathways include involvement of Heart Team discussions for complex multivessel diseases.
Use of CABG or staged PCI based on complexity and Heart Team recommendations.
Guideline-Directed Medical Therapy (GDMT) considered in non-complex cases.
footnotes: Decisions should consider renal function, lesion anatomy, and patient comorbidities.
Heart Team discussions ensure suitable decision-making.
Staged PCI can be performed in hospital or post-discharge.

Patient Clinical Status Definitions.Elective: The patient's cardiac function has been stable in the days or weeks before intervention. The intervention can be deferred without increased risk of compromise to cardiac outcome.
Patient Clinical Status Definitions.Urgent: Intervention is required during the same hospitalization to minimize chance of further clinical deterioration.
Patient Clinical Status Definitions.Emergency: Patients have ongoing, refractory cardiac compromise not responsive to any form of therapy except cardiac intervention.
Patient Clinical Status Definitions.Emergency/Salvage: Patients require resuscitation en route to the operating room or procedure room.

Recommendations for Coronary Angiography.Recommendation: In patients with NSTE-ACS at elevated risk of ischemic events who are candidates for revascularization, an invasive strategy is indicated.
In patients with NSTE-ACS and cardiogenic shock who are candidates, emergency revascularization is recommended to reduce death risk.
In patients with NSTE-ACS with refractory instability, an immediate invasive strategy is indicated to improve outcomes.
In NSTE-ACS patients stabilized at high clinical event risk, an early invasive strategy is reasonable to improve outcomes.
In NSTE-ACS patients stabilized at low risk, an invasive strategy before hospital discharge is reasonable to improve outcomes.
In NSTE-ACS patients with failed PCI and ischemia or hemodynamic compromise, emergency CABG is reasonable.
In NSTE-ACS with cardiogenic shock, routine multivessel PCI of non-culprit lesions should not be performed.

content.Title: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Coronary Revascularization Guideline
Key Points: The guideline discusses early versus delayed invasive strategies in patients with NSTE-ACS.
Early invasive strategy is recommended for cardiogenic shock, refractory angina, or high-risk patients within 24 hours.
Invasive strategy with intent to perform revascularization is recommended before hospital discharge.
Retrospective reviews have shown a reduced mortality rate with emergency CABG in ongoing ischemia or hemodynamic compromise.
CULPRIT-SHOCK trial indicated that PCI with staged revascularization of non-culprit lesions results in better outcomes.
No benefit of immediate multivessel PCI in NSTEMI was found from the CULPRIT-SHOCK trial.

Strategies: Immediate invasive strategy for cardiogenic shock or hemodynamic instability.
Early invasive strategy (within 24 hours) for high-risk patients.
Planned invasive strategy for stable patients prior to discharge.

content.Title: Revascularization in SIHD

Recommendations.Type: Left ventricular dysfunction and multivessel CAD

Recommendations.Details: CABG is recommended to improve survival in patients with SIHD and multivessel CAD with severe left ventricular systolic dysfunction (ejection fraction <35%).
CABG including a left internal mammary artery graft to the LAD is reasonable to improve survival in patients with mild-to-moderate left ventricular systolic dysfunction (ejection fraction 35%-50%).
CABG is recommended to improve survival in patients with significant left main stenosis.
PCI is reasonable to improve survival in selected patients for whom PCI can provide equivalent revascularization to that possible with CABG.
CABG may be reasonable to improve survival in patients with significant stenosis in 3 major coronary arteries (with or without proximal LAD) and suitable anatomy.
The usefulness of PCI to improve survival is uncertain in patients with significant stenosis in 3 major coronary arteries suitable for PCI.

Recommendations.Type: Stenosis in the proximal LAD artery

Recommendations.Details: The usefulness of coronary revascularization to improve survival is uncertain in patients with normal left ventricular ejection fraction and stenosis in the proximal LAD.

Recommendations.Type: Single- or double-vessel disease not involving the proximal LAD

Recommendations.Details: Coronary revascularization is not recommended to improve survival in patients with normal left ventricular ejection fraction, 1- or 2-vessel CAD involving the proximal LAD.
Coronary revascularization should not be performed solely to improve survival in patients with ≥1 coronary arteries not anatomically or functionally significant.

content.Synopsis: Studies show CABG offers survival benefit over medical therapy in multiple patient subsets, including those with left main CAD, triple-vessel CAD, and ischemic cardiomyopathy. The benefits of PCI vs medical therapy are subject to clinical decisions weighing risk and suitability.

content.Context: Evaluation of recent data to assess the need for revascularization procedures in specific patient populations

content.Title: Revascularization in Patients with SIHD
content.Description: This flowchart outlines the decision-making process for revascularization in patients with stable ischemic heart disease (SIHD). It addresses indications to improve symptoms and anatomic indications to improve survival.
Key Points: Indications include refractory angina on medical therapy, left main disease, and multivessel CAD.
Options for treatment include CABG, PCI, and GDMT.
Decisions are influenced by factors like significant left main stenosis, ischemic cardiomyopathy, suitability for CABG, and ejection fraction (EF).
Use of heart team discussions for complex cases is encouraged.

content.Synopsis: Clinical practice guidelines include recommendations for revascularization in SIHD patients based on CABG or PCI to improve survival or reduce symptoms compared to medical therapy alone. Several studies suggest revascularization lowers the risk of adverse events such as cardiac death, MI, or urgent revascularization compared with medical therapy alone.

Recommendations.1: In patients with SIHD and multivessel CAD appropriate for either CABG or PCI, revascularization is recommended to reduce the risk of cardiovascular events such as spontaneous MI, unplanned urgent revascularizations, or cardiac death.

Supporting Evidence: The MASS II study found lower 10-year rates of cardiac death after CABG or PCI than after medical therapy alone.
A meta-analysis of 25 studies enrolling 19,806 patients showed reduced overall MI following revascularization.
A dedicated RCT found no survival advantage for CABG or PCI over medical therapy in certain settings.
The ISCHEMIA trial found no difference in event rates in certain patients with multivessel CAD between CABG or PCI versus medical therapy.
content.Clinical Principle: The more myocardium at risk, the greater the survival advantage of revascularization over medical therapy.

recommendations.text: In patients with refractory angina despite medical therapy and with significant coronary artery stenoses amenable to revascularization, revascularization is recommended to improve symptoms.
In patients with angina but no anatomic or physiological criteria for revascularization, neither CABG nor PCI should be performed.

content.synopsis: One of the main goals of coronary revascularization with either PCI or CABG surgery is to improve symptoms. Medical therapy can often be effective, but revascularization offers greater improvement in angina or quality of life than does medical therapy alone. However, inappropriate revascularization of nonobstructive plaques can lead to progression of CAD and inappropriate PCI can cause peri-procedural MIs without improving outcomes. In patients requiring revascularization for significant left main CAD with high-complexity CAD, CABG over PCI is recommended to improve survival.
content.recommendation_support: Multiple RCTs confirm that revascularization improves anginal symptoms more than optimal medical therapy. Results from trials like ORBITA questioned the placebo effect, while ISCHEMIA showed relevant improvement.

content.summary: The guideline discusses coronary artery disease (CAD) revascularization in patients with diabetes. It evaluates coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG) and percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI). CABG often shows better survival rates, especially in patients with complex anatomy. The SYNTAX score is used to assess disease complexity, affecting treatment decisions. For patients with diabetes and multivessel CAD, CABG is preferred if they have involvement of the left anterior descending artery (LAD) and are suitable candidates. PCI is considered for poor surgical candidates.
recommendations: CABG is recommended over PCI for patients with diabetes and multivessel CAD with LAD involvement.
PCI can be useful for poor surgical candidates with multivessel CAD.
PCI may be considered for patients with left main stenosis and low to intermediate complexity disease.

content.title: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Coronary Revascularization Guideline
content.recommendation_summary: Guidelines for patients with previous Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting (CABG) and considerations for Percutaneous Coronary Intervention (PCI) and CABG in diabetes patients.

key_recommendations.recommendation: In patients with previous CABG with a patent LIMA to the LAD who need repeat revascularization, if PCI is feasible, it is reasonable to choose PCI over CABG.

In patients with previous CABG and refractory angina on GDMT that is attributable to LAD disease, it is reasonable to choose CABG over PCI when an internal mammary artery (IMA) can be used as a conduit to the LAD.
In patients with previous CABG and complex CAD, it may be reasonable to choose CABG over PCI when an IMA can be used as a conduit to the LAD.

clinical_trials.name: FREEDOM
clinical_trials.focus: Comparing CABG with PCI exclusively in 1900 patients with diabetes.

clinical_trials.name: EXCEL
clinical_trials.focus: Large RCT exclusively enrolled patients with left main CAD.

key_points: High surgical risk patients with diabetes are more likely to require PCI in current practice.
No significant statistical difference in cardiovascular mortality rate between PCI and CABG groups.

Recommendation: In patients with multivessel CAD amenable to treatment with either PCI or CABG who are unable to access, tolerate, or adhere to DAPT for the appropriate duration of treatment, CABG is reasonable in preference to PCI.
content.Synopsis: Patients undergoing coronary revascularization should consider factors that affect adherence to medications. Premature cessation of DAPT after PCI is associated with stent thrombosis and poor outcomes. Risk factors for stent thrombosis include patient-specific, lesion-specific, and treatment-specific factors. Early DAPT interruption is a reversible risk factor for stent thrombosis. CABG is a safe option for patients not likely to adhere to DAPT.
Supporting Information: Stent thrombosis after PCI can lead to large-territory MI and poor outcomes. Early DAPT interruption can significantly increase the risk of stent thrombosis.

content.Title: Revascularization in Pregnant and Older Patients

Recommendations.Description: In pregnant patients with STEMI not caused by spontaneous coronary artery dissection (SCAD), it is reasonable to perform primary PCI as the preferred revascularization strategy.
In pregnant patients with NSTE-ACS, an invasive strategy is reasonable if medical therapy is ineffective for the management of life-threatening complications.

Sections.Synopsis: Pregnant patients require a multidisciplinary approach to determine appropriate coronary revascularization treatment. Use of antiplatelet agents during pregnancy, especially in the third trimester, should be cautious. Clopidogrel should be used for the shortest duration possible if needed.
Supportive Text: Coronary revascularization in pregnant patients with STEMI is typically via PCI, with CABG performed when necessary.
Invasive treatment is viable if medical therapy fails in NSTE-ACS cases.

Recommendation.Description: In older adults, as in all patients, the treatment strategy for CAD should be based on an individual's preferences, cognitive function, and life expectancy.

Sections.Synopsis: Older patients present a unique challenge for revascularization due to complex health conditions and high comorbidity rates. The approach should be individualized.

Recommendations.Description: In patients with CKD undergoing contrast media injection for coronary angiography, measures should be taken to minimize the risk of contrast-induced acute kidney injury (AKI).
In patients with STEMI and CKD, coronary angiography and revascularization are recommended, with adequate measures to reduce the risk of AKI.
In high-risk patients with NSTE-ACS and CKD, it is reasonable to perform coronary angiography and revascularization, with adequate measures to reduce the risk of AKI.
In low-risk patients with NSTE-ACS and CKD, it is reasonable to weigh the risk of coronary angiography and revascularization against the potential benefit.
In asymptomatic patients with stable CAD and CKD, routine angiography and revascularization are not recommended if there is no compelling indication.

content.Synopsis: Patients with CKD constitute a growing subset of the population and have worse outcomes after AMI or PCI. Risk of cardiovascular death has been correlated with impaired renal function (glomerular filtration rate). Preexisting CKD is the strongest independent risk factor for AKI, linked with increased mortality. Strategies include adequate hydration, minimizing contrast media, and use of high-dose statins to reduce AKI risk.

Best Practices for CKD Patients Undergoi: Assess risk of contrast-induced AKI before procedure.
Record and minimize volume of contrast media.
Do not provide prophylactic renal replacement therapy.
Delay CABG in stable patients beyond 24 hours after angiography if feasible.
Clinical Insights: AKI prevention has limited clinical benefit in some trials.
Revascularization is recommended for STEMI patients despite CKD due to reduced mortality.
Observational studies show a lower mortality rate for invasive strategies in NSTE-ACS with CKD.
Risk-reduction from invasive strategies is often seen in high-risk subgroups.
The ISCHEMIA-CKD trial observed no improved outcomes for invasive strategies versus conservative ones for moderate CKD with ischemia.
Recommendation for Noncardiac Surgery: Patients with non-left main or noncomplex CAD undergoing noncardiac surgery should not receive routine coronary revascularization just to reduce perioperative cardiovascular events.

content.title: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Coronary Revascularization Guideline
content.summary: This guideline provides recommendations for coronary revascularization to reduce ventricular arrhythmias. It highlights the lack of benefit for routine revascularization before vascular surgery and suggests specific recommendations for patients with ventricular arrhythmias.

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with ventricular fibrillation, polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (VT), or cardiac arrest, revascularization of significant CAD is recommended to improve survival.
In patients with CAD and suspected scar-mediated sustained monomorphic VT, revascularization is not recommended for the sole purpose of preventing recurrent VT.

supportive_text: The CARP study found no difference in 30-day and 1-year rates of death or MI with PCI or CABG compared to medical therapy.
Revascularization may reduce the burden of polymorphic VT and ventricular fibrillation, resulting in improved survival.
Monomorphic VT in nonacute settings is usually due to scar-related reentry.
content.synopsis: Evaluation for potential ischemic CAD is crucial in patients with ventricular arrhythmias. Revascularization in life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias and survivors of cardiac arrest is linked to reduced arrhythmia and improved survival, especially with CABG or PCI.

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with SCAD who have hemodynamic instability or ongoing ischemia despite conservative therapy, revascularization may be considered if feasible.
Routine revascularization for SCAD should not be performed.

content.synopsis: SCAD is characterized by the interruption of the coronary artery intimal layer and intramural hematoma, causing vessel compression, and typically presenting as an ACS. A notable subset is associated with ongoing symptomatic ischemia, which can progress to complete occlusion. Treatment of patients with SCAD is challenging, and guidance from randomized trials is lacking. Selective revascularization may be necessary in certain scenarios.
recommendation_support: SCAD may often heal without intervention, but patients with ischemia, occlusion, or instability may need urgent revascularization.
A study of patients with SCAD and presenting with STEMI showed better survival without revascularization compared to PCI.
Several retrospective studies suggest higher rates of cardiovascular events in patients who had a first-line revascularization strategy.

content.Synopsis: In patients after orthotopic heart transplantation, cardiac allograft vasculopathy presents a treatment challenge. It is a major cause of death following the first year. It is characterized by diffuse intimal hyperplasia. Limited treatment options include retransplantation. PCI serves as a palliative treatment in patients with focal disease. DES is favored over BMS for native CAD but was not clearly beneficial for cardiac allograft vasculopathy.

Recommendations.Recommendation: 1. In patients with ACS undergoing PCI, a radial approach is preferred to reduce the risk of death, vascular complications, or bleeding.
2. In patients with SIHD undergoing PCI, the radial approach is recommended to reduce access site bleeding and vascular complications.

content.General: Radial artery catheterization and PCI have gained popularity for their advantages such as faster ambulation and fewer complications. Experienced operators are recommended.

content.Transradial Access vs Femoral Access: The MATRIX trial showed a significantly lower rate of primary endpoint of net adverse clinical events in transradial compared to transfemoral access. This was driven by a lower rate of bleeding events and a lower 30-day mortality rate.
content.SAFARI-STEMI Trial: Demonstrated no significant difference in 30-day mortality between radial and femoral access.
content.Recommendation for Choice of Stent Type: DES should be used over BMS to prevent restenosis, MI, or acute stent thrombosis.
content.DES Developments: Newer-generation DES have higher efficacy and safety. Durables are ranked as durable-polymer DES > biodegradable-polymer DES > BMS based on safety.

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients undergoing coronary stent implantation, IVUS can be useful for procedural guidance, particularly in cases of left main or complex coronary artery stenting, to reduce ischemic events.
In patients undergoing coronary stent implantation, OCT is a reasonable alternative to IVUS for procedural guidance, except in ostial left main disease.
In patients with stent failure, IVUS or OCT is reasonable to determine the mechanism of stent failure.

content.Synopsis: Intracoronary imaging is useful in guiding coronary stent implantation in cases involving the left main artery or complex lesions, allowing full-thickness visibility and assessment of plaque and stent conditions. IVUS and OCT provide different advantages, with IVUS not requiring contrast media while OCT requires it but offers higher resolution.
Studies: Meta-analyses on IVUS-guided PCI showed a reduction in major adverse cardiac events (MACE).
The ILUMIEN study compared OCT and IVUS for peri-procedural guidance and showed noninferiority of OCT.
The DOCTORS trial showed that OCT-guided PCI resulted in improved post-PCI FFR.

content.Title: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Coronary Revascularization Guideline

Sections.Title: Thromboctomy

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with STEMI, routine aspiration thrombectomy before primary PCI is not useful.

Synopsis: Patients with STEMI have thrombotic occlusion of the infarct artery.
No clinical benefit from routine thrombolytic thrombectomy in PCI.
Initial studies showed improved myocardial blush grades and ST-segment-elevation resolution.
Larger studies show no benefit with thrombus aspiration.
Findings: No benefit in reduction of infarct size or improvement in cardiovascular outcomes.
A patient-level meta-analysis showed no significant reduction in cardiovascular death but did show a higher risk of stroke with routine aspiration thrombectomy.
Thrombus aspiration in high thrombus burden patients showed reduced cardiovascular death and increased stroke risk.

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with fibrotic or heavily calcified lesions, plaque modification with rotational atherectomy can be useful to improve procedural success.

In patients with fibrotic or heavily calcified lesions, plaque modification with orbital atherectomy, balloon atherectomy, laser angioplasty, or intracoronary lithotripsy may be considered to improve procedural success.

References: Referenced studies and additional information are available in Online Data Supplement 26 and 27.

content.Synopsis: Fibrotic or heavily calcified lesions can hinder stent expansion. Calcium deposits thicker than 500 μm or involving an arc of the vessel >270° predict the need for lesion modification. Lesions can be modified by rotational atherectomy, orbital atherectomy, cutting balloon atherotomy, intracoronary lithotripsy, or excimer laser angioplasty. Randomized trials show that routine use of athereolytic devices does not improve clinical outcomes. However, these devices may enhance procedural success in specific circumstances.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.Recommendation: Rotational atherectomy uses a diamond-tipped burr and is associated with restenosis and lumen loss. Trials have shown enhanced stent delivery and expansion in calcified vessels compared to balloons or cutting/sculpting balloons.

Orbital atherectomy is similar to rotational atherectomy and is indicated for inelastic lesions that cannot be dilated with balloons. Excimer laser coronary angioplasty uses a photo-acoustic mechanism helpful for calcified lesions or nonexpandable stents.

Recommendations: In patients with previous CABG undergoing PCI of a SVG, using an embolic protection device is recommended to decrease distal embolization risk.
In patients with previous CABG, if PCI of a diseased native coronary artery is feasible, it is recommended to choose PCI of the native artery over SVG.
In patients with chronic occlusion of a SVG, percutaneous revascularization is not recommended.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Embolic protection devices are used to prevent distal embolization. The SAFER trial showed that these devices can decrease SVG PCI complications.

content.Outcomes: SVG PCI is associated with high MACE, procedural MI risk, no-reflow, stent thrombosis, and ischemia-driven revascularization in the long-term.

content.Summary: This section of the guideline discusses the use of protection devices during percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) and the outcomes of using native arteries versus saphenous vein grafts (SVG) in coronary artery disease patients undergoing PCI. It mentions that no randomized studies compare the outcomes between PCI of diseased native arteries and PCI of SVGs. Observational studies have shown mixed results, and the use of embotic protection devices is debated. Regarding chronic total occlusion (CTO), the guideline notes the rarity of randomized trials proving the benefit of PCI in terms of cardiac outcomes.

Recommendations.Details: In patients with suitable anatomy who have refractory angina on medical therapy, after treatment of non-CTO lesions, the benefit of PCI of a CTO to improve symptoms is uncertain.

content.Synopsis: Chronic total occlusion (CTO) is observed in about a quarter of coronary angiography patients. Technical advances have led to high success rates in interventions, although mortality after CTO PCI is low, complications occur in a significant minority. Procedural enthusiasm stems from retrospective data but RCTs have not supported meaningful improvement.

content.Optimal Medical Therapy for Chronic Tota: Optimal medical therapy for the treatment of chronic total occlusions indicated a reduced frequency of angina and improved quality of life. However, further trials are suggested to explore more definitive endpoints.

Recommendations for Patients with Stent.Recommendation: Use DES for patients with clinical in-stent restenosis undergoing repeat PCI to improve outcomes if conditions allow.
Consider CABG for patients with recurrent diffuse ISR requiring revascularization.
Use brachytherapy for patients with recurrent ISR to improve symptoms.

content.Synopsis: Usage of newer-generation DES has reduced ISR risk compared to older stents. Neointimal hyperplasia after stent intervention remains the main mechanism of ISR. Various treatments have been explored including angioplasty, CABG, and vascular brachytherapy, with DES being more beneficial. ISR treatment should be individualized.

recommendations.recommendation: In selected high-risk patients, elective insertion of an appropriate hemodynamic support device as an adjunct to PCI may be reasonable to prevent hemodynamic compromise during PCI.

content.synopsis: Patients undergoing complex PCI are at risk of hypotension, decompensated heart failure, shock, or arrhythmias that may lead to rapid hemodynamic deterioration or death. Intra-aortic balloon pump counterpulsation provides minimal hemodynamic support for PCI but improves coronary and cerebral perfusion. New hemodynamic support devices are undergoing evaluation in clinical trials.
recommendation_specific_supporting_text: The routine use of hemodynamic support devices for complex PCI has not been shown to reduce cardiovascular events.
In the BCIS-1 trial, there was no difference in the primary composite outcome with intra-aortic balloon counterpulsation.

recommendations: In patients undergoing PCI, a loading dose of aspirin, followed by daily dosing, is recommended to reduce ischemic events.
In patients with ACS undergoing PCI, a loading dose of a P2Y12 inhibitor, followed by daily dosing, is recommended to reduce ischemic events.
In patients with SIHD undergoing PCI, a loading dose of clopidogrel, followed by daily dosing, is recommended to reduce ischemic events.
In patients undergoing PCI within 24 hours after fibrinolytic therapy, a loading dose of 300 mg of clopidogrel, followed by daily dosing, is recommended to reduce ischemic events.

DAPT with aspirin and oral P2Y12 inhibit.PLATO trial: examined the use of low-dose aspirin with ticagrelor and suggested chewable aspirin may be preferable.
DAPT with aspirin and oral P2Y12 inhibit.A loading dose of clopidogrel is necessa: Essential for patients undergoing PCI. Starting with a loading dose offers best protection against ischemic events, especially when used promptly post-procedure.

content.section_title: Oral and Parenteral Antiplatelet Agents for Patients Undergoing PCI

clinical_studies.name: CLARITY
clinical_studies.description: Clopidogrel resulted in a 46% reduction in rate of cardiovascular death or recurrent MI or stroke at 30 days among patients referred for PCI.

clinical_studies.name: TRITON-TIMI 38
clinical_studies.description: Prasugrel reduced the rate of the composite endpoint of death from vascular causes, MI, or stroke compared with clopidogrel.

clinical_studies.name: PLATO
clinical_studies.description: No significant differences in study-defined bleeding events with ticagrelor compared to clopidogrel, though non-CABG major bleeding was higher with ticagrelor.

clinical_studies.name: TREAT
clinical_studies.description: Support the use of ticagrelor over clopidogrel in patients with STEMI treated with fibrinolytic therapy.

notes: Ticagrelor and prasugrel not studied for long-term clinical outcomes in patients with SIHD undergoing PCI.
Older patients may require individualized loading doses based on risk of bleeding.

recommendations.recommendation: In patients undergoing PCI who are P2Y12 inhibitor naïve, intravenous cangrelor may be reasonable to reduce periprocedural ischemic events.
In patients with ACS undergoing PCI with large thrombus burden, no-reflow, or slow flow, intravenous glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor agents are reasonable to improve procedural success.
In patients with SIHD undergoing PCI, the routine use of an intravenous glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor agent is not recommended.

content.synopsis: Cangrelor is a potent, direct, reversible, short-acting intravenous P2Y12 inhibitor. It is used for rapid onset of platelet inhibition in patients who have not been pretreated with a P2Y12 inhibitor and is compared favorably with clopidogrel.
content.key_studies: CHAMPION trials showed lower rates of stent thrombosis and death with cangrelor compared to clopidogrel.

content.Synopsis: Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptor inhibitors are drugs that target the glycoprotein IIb/IIIa platelet receptor. The use of these inhibitors in conjunction with PCI has not shown improved outcomes and may increase bleeding. These inhibitors may be reserved for specific cases with large thrombus burden.

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients undergoing PCI, administration of UFH is useful to reduce ischemic events.
In patients with heparin-induced thrombocytopenia undergoing PCI, bivalirudin or argatroban should replace UFH.
In patients undergoing PCI, bivalirudin may reduce bleeding.
In patients treated with enoxaparin, intravenous enoxaparin can be considered during PCI.
Subcutaneous enoxaparin should not be used for PCI if administered within 12 hours prior, to avoid increased bleeding.

content.Antithrombotic Therapy: Includes UFH, bivalirudin, and enoxaparin. Fondaparinux is not recommended alone in PCI.

UFH.Additional Dosing: 2000-5000 U to achieve an ACT of 250-300 s
UFH.No Prior Anticoagulant Therapy: 70-100 U/kg initial bolus to achieve target ACT

With Prior Treatment.0.3 mg/kg IV Recommended: If last SC dose within 8 hrs and additional not needed

Bivalirudin.In Therapeutic Range: 0.75 mg/kg IV bolus, 1.75 mg/kg/h IV infusion
Bivalirudin.No UFH History: 0.75 mg/kg IV bolus, 1.75 mg/kg/h IV infusion

Argatroban.Prior Therapy: 350 µg/kg bolus, then 15 µg/kg/min IV infusion
Argatroban.Without Prior Therapy: 200 µg/kg IV bolus, then 15 µg/kg/min IV infusion

RCT Highlights.Bivalirudin vs Heparin: Reported no difference but less bleeding with bivalirudin

PCI Highlight.Pre-PCI Enoxaparin: Avoid UFH if administered within past 12 hours

content.Title: General Procedural Issues for CABG
content.Section: 12.1 Perioperative Considerations in Patients Undergoing CABG
content.Recommendation: For patients undergoing CABG, establishment of multidisciplinary, evidence-based perioperative management programs is recommended to optimize analgesia, minimize opioid exposure, prevent complications and to reduce time to extubation, length of stay, and health care costs.
content.Synopsis: Emphasizes the use of fast-track cardiac anesthesia and multidisciplinary perioperative programs to improve outcomes after CABG. These programs utilize short-acting anesthetic agents and targeted monitoring modalities.
Key Points: Fast-track cardiac anesthesia is crucial for improving outcomes.
Enhanced recovery programs focus on minimizing opioid exposure and reducing extubation time.
Use of specific anesthetic and monitoring modalities is recommended.
Intraoperative mechanical ventilation strategies and non-opioid medications can benefit outcomes.

Table 11 Highlights.Perioperative analgesia: Use of nonopioid medications and regional techniques to reduce opioid use.
Table 11 Highlights.Maintenance anesthesia: Choice of intravenous versus volatile agents.
Table 11 Highlights.Goal-directed therapy: Protocols for fluids and vasopressors targeting specific hemodynamic goals.
Table 11 Highlights.TEE and CABG: Intraoperative TEE for assessment of cardiac functions and decision-making.

Pulmonary artery catheter.High-risk surgery: Highly selective use of pulmonary artery catheters for high-risk patients (older, with congestive heart failure, pulmonary hypertension, or previous multiple valve procedures) may be safe and may potentially aid in the surveillance and treatment of hemodynamic instability.
Pulmonary artery catheter.Low-risk surgery: The use of pulmonary artery catheters in low-risk or clinically stable patients is discouraged as it is associated with increased interventions without associated improvement in morbidity or mortality rates.

CNS monitoring.Cerebral oxygen saturation: Intraoperative monitoring of cerebral oxygen saturation is shown to guide anesthetic decision-making and may prevent postoperative neurocognitive dysfunction.
CNS monitoring.Processed electroencephalogram: Routine use of intraoperative monitoring of processed electroencephalogram has yielded inconsistent results with respect to the prevention of recall, determination of depth of anesthesia, or improvement in rate of recovery after cardiac surgery.

Bypass Conduits in Patients Undergoing C.Use of Radial Artery: In patients undergoing isolated CABG, use of a radial artery is recommended over a saphenous vein conduit to improve long-term cardiac outcomes.
Bypass Conduits in Patients Undergoing C.Use of IMA: In patients undergoing CABG, IMA should be used to bypass the LAD to improve survival and reduce recurrent ischemic events.
Bypass Conduits in Patients Undergoing C.Use of BIMA: In patients undergoing CABG, bilateral IMA (BIMA) grafting may be beneficial to improve long-term cardiac outcomes.

content.Overview: The guideline discusses the addition of CABG (Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting) to cardiac surgeries and its impact on patients with and without significant CAD (Coronary Artery Disease). It covers the risks, benefits, and recommendations regarding CABG, including age-related risks, postoperative outcomes, and the incorporation of CABG with other cardiac operations such as AVR (Aortic Valve Replacement).
content.Key Recommendations: 1. Routine use of epi-aortic ultrasound scanning in patients undergoing CABG is recommended to reduce atheroembolic complications. 2. Limited data on CABG in intermediate CAD, but testing with iFR or FFR may help guide decision-making.
content.Supporting Evidence: Studies show CABG increases the risk of perioperative morbidity but improves long-term survival and quality of life. Observational studies have demonstrated the impact of CABG on postoperative outcomes, including mortality reduction.
content.Epi-aortic Ultrasound: Recognized as the gold standard for the detection of atherosclerosis, epi-aortic ultrasound is superior to digital palpation or echocardiography and has been associated with better operative outcomes.

content.Title: Use of Cardiopulmonary Bypass in Patients Undergoing CABG

Recommendations.Guideline: In patients with significant calcification of the aorta, the use of techniques to avoid aortic manipulation (off-pump techniques or beating heart) is reasonable to decrease the incidence of perioperative stroke when performed by experienced surgeons.
In patients with significant pulmonary disease, off-pump surgery may be reasonable to reduce perioperative risk when performed by experienced surgeons.

content.Synopsis: When planning operative strategy for CABG, risks of aortic manipulation need consideration. Alternative operative strategies like off-pump or beating heart approaches are suggested to minimize complications, particularly in the presence of a calcified ascending aorta and pulmonary disease. Off-pump CABG potentially reduces immediate short-term risks such as stroke, blood transfusion, extubation, and mechanical ventilation.

Additional Recommendations.Guideline: For patients undergoing CABG, an intraoperative continuous insulin infusion is recommended to maintain serum glucose level <180 mg/dL to reduce sternal wound infection.

content.Synopsis: Sternal wound infection has become less common in CABG surgery, with current rates reported to be less than 1%. However, the associated risk of death may increase several-fold. Management of hyperglycemia with perioperative insulin infusion to maintain a glucose level <180 mg/dL is important to prevent infection, improve survival, and reduce ischemic events. Continuous intravenous insulin reduces postoperative complications such as mediastinitis, cardiac arrhythmias, deep sternal wound infections, renal failure, and length of stay. Additional strategies include antibiotic prophylaxis and other measures.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Continuous intravenous infusion of insulin is effective in maintaining blood glucose <180 mg/dL in patients undergoing CABG to reduce the risk of sternal wound infection.

The optimal level of glycemic control remains controversial. Some studies show that maintaining glucose levels between 100 mg/dL and 140 mg/dL may not reduce complications versus a higher target of up to 180 mg/dL.

RCTs have found that continuous intravenous insulin is associated with reduced variability in glucose concentration, reduced hospital length of stay, and reduced ischemic events compared to subcutaneous insulin.
Aggressive preventive approaches, including antibiotics and nasal carriers' treatment, show varying levels of evidence for reducing infections.
RCTs comparing intensive and conventional treatment have found no significant differences in outcomes.

Best Practices to Reduce Sternal Wound I: Perform nasal swab testing for Staphylococcus aureus.
Apply mupirocin 2% ointment to known nasal carriers of S aureus.
Apply preoperative intranasal mupirocin 2% ointment to patients with unknown nasal culture or PCR result.
Redose prophylactic antimicrobials for long procedures or in cases of excessive blood loss during CABG.
Treat all distant extrathoracic infections before nonemergency surgical coronary revascularization.
Advise smoking cessation before elective CABG surgery.
Apply topical antibiotics to the cut edges of the sternum during cardiac surgical procedures.
Use skeletonized harvest of IMA in BIMA grafting.
Do not continue prophylactic antibiotics beyond 48 hours.
Antiplatelet Therapy Recommendations: Patients already taking aspirin should continue until the time of surgery.
For urgent CABG, clopidogrel and ticagrelor should be discontinued at least 24 hours before surgery.
Discontinue short-acting glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitors before surgery to reduce bleeding risk.
For elective CABG, discontinue clopidogrel 5 days, ticagrelor 3 days, and prasugrel 7 days before surgery.
Do not initiate aspirin in patients not already taking it in the immediate preoperative period.

General Information.Details: Continuation of aspirin until the time of surgery is likely associated with a reduction in myocardial infarction (MI) but increases the risk of perioperative bleeding and transfusion.
The administration of aspirin and a P2Y12 receptor inhibitor (e.g., clopidogrel, ticagrelor, prasugrel) is common, particularly in the setting of ACS or recent stent placement.
These inhibitors (e.g., eptifibatide, tirofiban, abciximab) are sometimes given to patients who are at high risk of acute ischemic events while awaiting CABG.
CABG performed <5 days after the discontinuation of clopidogrel is associated with an increased risk of major bleeding complications.
Experience suggests that preoperative ticagrelor should be withheld to reduce bleeding and blood product administration.
Timing for prasugrel is less established, with higher major bleeding risk if surgery is performed early (<3 days).

General Information.Aspect: Aspirin Therapy in Immediate Preoperative Period
General Information.Details: Initiation of aspirin therapy in the immediate preoperative period (<24 hours) was linked to increased episodes of major bleeding, but no significant differences were found in major cardiovascular events.

Beta Blockers.Recommendation: Patients without contraindication to beta blockers should receive them before surgery to reduce postoperative atrial fibrillation and potentially to reduce in-hospital and 30-day mortality rates.
The role of preoperative beta blockers in acute postoperative myocardial ischemia and ventricular arrhythmia is uncertain.

content.Synopsis: In patients undergoing elective CABG, the risks and benefits of beta-blocker or amiodarone administration before surgery should be carefully considered. Preoperative beta blockers are associated with reduced incidence of postoperative atrial fibrillation, but recent data show mixed results for other outcomes like death and arrhythmias.
content.Recommendation-Specific Supporting Text: Small RCTs and meta-analyses have shown preoperative beta blockers reduce atrial fibrillation after CABG. However, the trials have limitations due to mixed arms and initiation timing. Preoperative prophylactic amiodarone decreases the incidence of atrial arrhythmias and stroke compared to placebo. Observational data on beta-blockers show mixed results in reducing hospital and 30-day mortality.
content.Pharmacotherapy in Patients After Revasc: Patients need aggressive secondary preventive measures which include lifestyle changes and medications to control cholesterol, blood sugar, and blood pressure, alongside antiplatelet therapies.
content.Recommendation for Dual Antiplatelet The: Shorter-duration DAPT (1-3 months) is recommended in selected patients undergoing PCI, with subsequent transition to P2Y12 inhibitor monotherapy to reduce bleeding risk.

genes.context: One study found that, when compared with no preoperative beta blockers, noncytochrome P4502D6 metabolized agents (i.e., atenolol and sotalol) were associated with a lower incidence of operative death; however, P4502D6 metabolized agents (i.e., metoprolol, propranolol, carvedilol, and labetalol) were not.

content.title: Use of DAPT for Patients After PCI
content.context: This guideline provides recommendations for dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) duration in patients undergoing percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI). The flowchart categorizes patients based on whether they have stable ischemic heart disease (SIHD) or acute coronary syndrome (ACS), and whether they receive drug-eluting stents (DES) or bare metal stents (BMS).

therapy_recommendations.SIHD_DES: At least 6 months aspirin plus clopidogrel
therapy_recommendations.SIHD_BMS: At least 1 month of aspirin plus clopidogrel
therapy_recommendations.ACS: At least 12 months of aspirin plus clopidogrel, or prasugrel, or ticagrelor

therapy_recommendations.discontinuation: Discontinuation of aspirin after 1-3 months with continued P2Y12 monotherapy may be reasonable if high risk of bleeding
Discontinuation of P2Y12 after 3 months may be reasonable if high risk of bleeding
Discontinuation of P2Y12 after 6 months may be reasonable if high risk of bleeding
therapy_recommendations.SIHD_DES_extension: More than 6 months of DAPT may be reasonable if no significant bleeding risk
therapy_recommendations.SIHD_BMS_extension: More than 3 months of DAPT may be reasonable if no significant bleeding risk

therapy_recommendations.ACS_extension: More than 1 year of DAPT may be reasonable if no significant bleeding risk

content.additional_notes: Use a Heart Team approach when decisions are unclear; consult Section 17 for questions and future directions.

content.Synopsis: After PCI, the use of DAPT prevents stent thrombosis and reduces ischemic events but increases bleeding. Recent studies suggest shorter-term DAPT (3-6 months) shows less bleeding and fewer ischemic events compared to longer-term DAPT (>12 months). The guidelines highlight recommendations for short and prolonged DAPT followed by aspirin monotherapy post-revascularization. Trials have shown 40% reduction in bleeding events with shorter-term DAPT followed by P2Y12 monotherapy.
content.Recommendations for Antiplatelet Therapy: 1. Patients undergoing CABG should receive aspirin postoperatively to reduce SVG closure and adverse cardiovascular events. 2. In selected patients with CABG, DAPT with aspirin and ticagrelor or clopidogrel for 1 year may improve vein graft patency.
content.CABG-specific Synopsis: DAPT therapy mechanisms in CABG patients differ due to bypassing of coronary tree and distinct pathophysiology from ACS. Surgical bleeding remains a concern particularly in perioperative periods.

content.summary: The guideline discusses recommendations for the use of beta blockers in patients after revascularization, particularly those with stable ischemic heart disease (SIHD) and normal left ventricular function. It concludes that the routine use of beta blockers is not beneficial in reducing cardiovascular events after complete revascularization. For patients after CABG (Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting), beta blockers are recommended to reduce the incidence of postoperative atrial fibrillation.

recommendations.topic: Beta Blockers in Patients After Revascularization
recommendations.recommendation: In patients with SIHD and normal left ventricular function, the routine use of chronic oral beta blockers is not beneficial to reduce cardiovascular events after complete revascularization.

recommendations.topic: Beta Blockers for the Prevention of Atrial Fibrillation After CABG
recommendations.recommendation: In patients after CABG, beta blockers are recommended and should be started as soon as possible to reduce the incidence or clinical sequelae of postoperative atrial fibrillation.

supportive_text: In patients with preserved left ventricular function, there is a lack of data supporting the routine use of beta blockers after revascularization.
Large meta-analyses and observational studies have not shown benefits of beta blockers in patients undergoing PCI for stable angina.

content.general: New-onset postoperative atrial fibrillation occurs in about 18% of patients after CABG and is associated with increased risk of stroke and mortality. Beta-blockers may reduce the incidence of atrial fibrillation and ventricular arrhythmias. Recommendations include discontinuing aspirin in patients with atrial fibrillation undergoing PCI and using anticoagulants like rivaroxaban or warfarin to reduce bleeding risks.

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with atrial fibrillation undergoing PCI on anticoagulants, discontinue aspirin 1 to 4 weeks after PCI and use non-vitamin K oral anticoagulant or warfarin.
In patients with atrial fibrillation undergoing PCI on DAPT or P2Y12 inhibitor, use non-vitamin K oral anticoagulant over warfarin.

recommendations: Patients who have undergone revascularization should be prescribed a comprehensive cardiac rehabilitation program to reduce death and hospital readmissions and improve the quality of life.
Patients should be educated about cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk factors and their modification.
content.synopsis: Cardiac rehabilitation is evidence-based, including education, behavior modification, and exercise training to improve prevention outcomes in CVD patients. It addresses comorbid conditions, monitors safety issues, and facilitates adherence to medical therapies. The rehabilitation may be center-based or home-based, each overcoming different participation barriers.
recommendation_specific_support: The safety and effectiveness of center-based cardiac rehabilitation are well-established with reductions in hospital readmissions, secondary events, and deaths.
Core components of home-based cardiac rehabilitation are similar to center-based programs.

content.section: 15.2 Smoking Cessation in Patients After Revascularization

recommendations.recommendation: In patients who use tobacco and have undergone coronary revascularization, a combination of behavioral interventions plus pharmacotherapy is recommended to maximize cessation and reduce adverse cardiac events.

In patients who use tobacco and have undergone coronary revascularization, smoking cessation interventions are recommended during hospitalization and should include supportive follow-up for at least 1 month after discharge to facilitate tobacco cessation and reduce morbidity and mortality.

content.synopsis: Tobacco use, especially cigarette smoking, is a major risk factor for cardiovascular morbidity and mortality. Continued smoking after revascularization is associated with adverse clinical outcomes, particularly stent thrombosis. Cessation therapies include behavioral and pharmacological interventions, such as varenicline, bupropion, and individual counseling. Varenicline has shown high efficacy among patients motivated to quit smoking.

content.Title: 15.3 Psychological Interventions in Patients After Revascularization

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients who have undergone coronary revascularization who have symptoms of depression, anxiety, or stress, treatment with cognitive behavioral therapy, psychological counseling, and/or pharmacological interventions is beneficial to improve quality of life and cardiac outcomes.
In patients who have undergone coronary revascularization, it may be reasonable to screen for depression and refer or treat when it is indicated to improve quality of life and recovery.

Synopsis: Cardiac events and coronary revascularization are distressing, leading to psychosocial morbidity.
Anxiety, depression, and stress are associated with poor adherence to health behaviors, compromised quality of life, and increased recurrent cardiac events.
20% of patients who undergo CABG remain depressed postoperatively.
Several psychological therapies are used for secondary prevention to improve outcomes, including relaxation, stress management, and cognitive behavioral therapy.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Escitalopram was more effective than placebo in reducing depression.
ENRICHD trial demonstrated therapy with counseling or SSRIs reduced depression but not event-free survival after 24 months.
Depression is a common comorbidity after revascularization.
Effective depression treatments, including depression screening and support systems, are highlighted.

Assessment of Outcomes.Recommendation: With the goal of improving patient outcomes, it is recommended that cardiac surgery and PCI programs participate in state, regional, or national clinical data registries and receive periodic reports of their risk-adjusted outcomes as a quality assessment and improvement strategy.

With the goal of improving patient outcomes, it is reasonable for cardiac surgery and PCI programs to have a quality improvement program that routinely reviews institutional quality programs and outcomes, reviews individual operator outcomes, provides peer review of difficult or complicated cases, and performs random case reviews.
Smaller volume cardiac surgery and PCI programs may consider affiliating with a high-volume center to improve patient care.

content.Synopsis: Centers that provide coronary revascularization should participate in clinical data registries with the intent to review and continuously improve patient outcomes. Comparison of outcomes through national databases allows for improvement in quality improvement initiatives.

Recommendation-Specific Supporting Text.1: Participation in registries that provide risk-adjusted outcomes is beneficial in quality assessment and improvement.
Recommendation-Specific Supporting Text.2: Quality and performance measures are defined by attributes related to structure, processes, and risk-adjusted outcomes.
Recommendation-Specific Supporting Text.3: Small-volume coronary revascularization programs may benefit from affiliation with larger volume programs.

content.Unanswered Questions and Future Directio: The indications for revascularization and the approach to treatment in patients with CAD are based on evidence supporting benefit or lack thereof.

Special Populations.Underrepresented Racial and Ethnic Group: There are persistent disparities in outcomes in CVD treatments. Recommendations are often derived from RCT data with skewed demographic representation.
Special Populations.Left Ventricular Dysfunction: While CABG improves outcomes, the effectiveness varies with myocardial viability.

17.2.General Information: The document discusses various conditions related to coronary revascularization, including SCAD, coronary artery aneurysm, myocardial bridging, and treatment of graft failure. Clinical guidelines emphasize cautious use of PCI for SCAD and limited robust data for treating graft failure. It also touches on considerations for antiplatelet therapy in patients with ACS after CABG, particularly with an indication for anticoagulation.

Sections.17.2.1: Insufficient data on the role of PCI in patients with left ventricular dysfunction to improve survival or cardiovascular outcomes.
Sections.17.2.2: SCAD is increasingly recognized as a cause of ACS in young patients, especially women. The management is cautious, with conservative treatment prevailing.
Sections.17.2.3: Coronary artery aneurysms are rare findings; no clear best treatment exists.
Sections.17.2.4: Myocardial Bridging can lead to ischemia, AMI, stress cardiomyopathy. Surgical approaches show promise in patients with ischemic pain.
Sections.17.2.5: Graft failure lacks robust recommendations. Consideration for intervention should involve a Heart Team.
Sections.17.2.6: Antiplatelet therapy after ACS and CABG requires careful consideration and further study.

17.3.Section 17.3.1: Use of radial artery for a conduit after radial artery catheterization is increasing.

content.CABG: The radial artery is preferred over LIMA if manipulated. Surgery benefits due to reduced acute and long-term graft patency.
content.Findings: Increased intimal thickness and medial dissections often noted after radial artery catheterization. Abnormal findings persist.
content.Further Research: Needed to determine healing and suitability for graft harvesting.
content.Multivessel Disease: Operators attempt to treat all vessels. No trials directly compare strategies. Outcomes worse with incomplete revascularization.
content.Hybrid Surgery: Combines off-pump LIMA with PCI. Shows similar rates of death and stroke compared to CABG. Popularity rising, especially in the US.
content.PCI Before TAVR: Variable CAD presence in TAVR patients. PCI often planned if proximal obstruction.

Revascularization Before Organ Transplan.RCTs: There are currently no RCTs evaluating the role of revascularization before solid organ transplantation.
Revascularization Before Organ Transplan.Vascular Surgery: The RCT of revascularization before vascular surgery did not report improved outcomes with PCI.
Revascularization Before Organ Transplan.Cardiovascular Events: Increased risk of cardiovascular events among renal transplant recipients is noted.
Revascularization Before Organ Transplan.Routine Risk Assessment: Performed before consideration for transplantation due to obstructive CAD.
Revascularization Before Organ Transplan.Transplantation Surgeons' Hesitation: Surgeons are hesitant due to the increased risk without revascularization.
Revascularization Before Organ Transplan.Reference to Patients: Common referral for revascularization in preparation for organ transplantation.
Revascularization Before Organ Transplan.Ischemia CKD Trial: No differences in outcomes with routine revascularization even with severe ischemia noted in Ischemia CKD trial.
Revascularization Before Organ Transplan.Waitlist: Only about 10% of enrolled patients were on the waitlist for transplantation.
Revascularization Before Organ Transplan.Conclusions: Uncertainty remains on whether revascularization before organ transplantation offers better outcomes. RCTs are needed.

context: One study found that, when compared with no preoperative beta blockers, noncytochrome P4502D6 metabolized agents (i.e., atenolol and sotalol) were associated with a lower incidence of operative death; however, P4502D6 metabolized agents (i.e., metoprolol, propranolol, carvedilol, and labetalol) were not.
